pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: MELAS Syndrome (Mitochondrial Encephalomyopathy, Lactic Acidosis, and Stroke-like episodes) THE SMOKING GUN: The simultaneous presentation of severe, intractable, normoammonemic Lactic Acidosis (greater than 10 mmol/L) concurrent with atypical, non-vascular territorial "stroke-like" lesions on MRI, particularly affecting the temporal and occipital cortices. Subsequent muscle biopsy revealed Ragged Red Fibers (RRFs), and genetic sequencing confirmed the common m.3243A>G point mutation in mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA). PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: MELAS is the most common mitochondrial disorder, typically caused by a mutation in the mitochondrial tRNA(Leu) gene (m.3243A>G). This mutation impairs protein synthesis within the mitochondria, severely compromising the function of the electron transport chain (Complex I and Complex IV). Tissues with high energy demands—namely the brain and skeletal muscle—are critically affected. The failure of oxidative phosphorylation forces cells to rely exclusively on anaerobic glycolysis, leading to massive lactic acid buildup (lactic acidosis) and subsequent neurological energy crisis. The "stroke-like" episodes are not true ischemic events but rather focal energy failure leading to cytotoxic edema, vasogenic changes, and eventually neuronal death. Treatment involves supportive care, aggressive management of seizures and acidosis, and potential supplementation with L-Arginine (which improves nitric oxide bioavailability and may mitigate endothelial dysfunction associated with the stroke-like episodes).
